California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


January 6, 1988




John R. Calhoun
City Attorney
City Hall
333 West Ocean Boulevard
Long Beach, CA  90802

Re:  88-017

Dear Mr. Calhoun:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act was received on January 4, 1988 by the Fair Political Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your advice request, you may contact me directly at (916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly. Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written advice. If more information is needed, we will contact you shortly to advise you as to the information needed. If your request is for informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can. (See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Adm. Code Sec. 18329).)

You also should be aware that your letter and our response are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

DMG:jaj


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

OFFICE OF THE

CITY ATTORNEY
OF

LONG BEACH

December 29, 1997

JOHN R. CALHOUN
CITY ATTORNEY

ROBERT E. SHANNON
ASSISTANT

MAIN OFFICE
City Hall
333 West Ocean Boulevard
Long Beach, California 90802
(213) 590-6061

HARBOR BRANCH OFFICE
Harbor Administration Building
Post Office Box 570
Long Beach, California 90801
(213) 590-4120

WORKERS' COMPENSATION SECTION
(213) 590-6583

Diane Griffith, General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Ms. Griffith:

Members of the Long Beach City Council have expressed concern regarding the applicability of the Political Reform Act of 1974 and regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission to Mr. Dan Rosenberg and/or the Grass Roots Action for Sane Planning (GRASP) organization which he purports to represent. Enclosed for your information and use are copies of three flyers Mr. Rosenberg recently distributed in the City of Long Beach which solicit contributions for what may be political purposes or activities regarding parking laws and regulations of the City of Long Beach.

In the past few weeks, Mr. Rosenberg has made several appearances before the City Council and has mentioned these flyers in his presentations.

Some of the obvious questions raised by this situation are as follows:

1. Is Mr. Rosenberg and/or the GRASP organization a committee under Section 82013 of the California Government Code?

2. Does money given pursuant to the solicitation contained in the flyers constitute a "contribution" under Government Code Section 82015; and does an expenditure of said funds constitute an "expenditure" under Section 82025 of the California Government Code?

3. What mechanism exists, if any, for determining when the $500 threshold set forth in Section 82013 has been reached or exceeded?

4. If any money received as a result of the subject solicitations by Rosenberg and/or GRASP constitutes a "contribution" under the Political Reform Act, does Section 84307 require that said funds be deposited in a separate account regardless whether the $500 threshold is reached?

Diane Griffith, General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
December 29, 1987
Page Two

     5. Is there any applicable FPPC rule or regu-
lation requiring any reports or accounting for
committees that receive or spend less than $500 in
contributions?

     It is requested that you review this matter and
provide our office with your comments and advice concerning
the applicability of the Political Reform Act and/or any of
the Commission's rules and regulations. The above references
to statutory provisions are merely intended to point out cer-
tain apparent questions raised by this matter, but please do
not restrict your review or comments to the code sections
mentioned.

     If you have any questions or need additional
information or desire to discuss this matter, please contact
me at (213) 590-6319.  Thank you for your consideration and
assistance.


                              JOHN R. CALHOUN
                              CITY ATTORNEY

cc:  Mayor and City Councilmembers

HOW TO STOP UNFAIR PARKING TICKETS: FLYERS ARE A CHEAPER THAN PARKING TICKETS

AS A RECENT FLYER, I PROMISED THAT WORKING
TOGETHER, WE WOULD "IMMEDIATELY SLOW DOWN THE
ISSUANCE OF UNFAIR PARKING TICKETS & THEN STOP
THEM COMPLETELY." I ALSO ASKED FOR YOUR HELP.
IT COSTS MONEY & ENERGY TO PRINT & DISTRIBUTE
THOUSANDS OF THESE FLYERS. I HAVE KEPT MY PROMISE
TO YOU. YOU MAY HAVE ALREADY NOTICED THAT
AS MORE OF THESE FLYERS BEGAN APPEARING ON YOUR
CAR, LESS PARKING TICKETS HAVE.(PARTICULAR, IF
OVERNIGHT TICKETS FOR PARKING TOO CLOSE TO THE
CORNER; OR A LITTLE BIT·IN THE RED; OR ON YOUR OWN LAWN; OR ACROSS YOUR OWN
DRIVEWAY, ETC. THESE FLYERS WORK BECAUSE THEY ACT AS A BRIGHT SPOTLIGHT ON
OFFICIAL ABUSE OF POWER & INSENSITIVITY TO OUR BASIC LEGITIMATE NEEDS. ONLY,
WHILE THE SPOTLIGHT CONTINUES TO SHINE BRIGHTLY CAN WE EXPECT THE CITY TO CONTINUE TO RELAX & REVIEW ENFORCEMENT OF PARKING RESTRICTIONS WHICH ARE NO LONGER FAIR OR REASONABLE UNDER PRESENT LIVING CONDITIONS WHERE YOU LIVE. ONLY
WITH YOUR HELP CAN I CONTINUE THESE EFFORTS. IF YOU BELIEVE THESE EFFORTS ARE
ALREADY SAVING SOME OF YOUR MONEY & CAN SAVE YOU LOT MORE OF IT IN THE FUTURE,
PLEASE HELP NOW! GRASS ROOTS ACTION FOR SANE PLANNING (GRASP), c/o DAN
ROSENBERG, 1325 E. 7TH ST., L.B. 90813 (591-5793)      12-1-87

STOP STREETSWEEPING PARKING TICKETS & FEEDING HUNGRY PARKING METERS (12/23-1/1)

SAY NO TO UNFAIR PARKING TICKETS & "GRASP" THE OPPORTUNITY TO GIVE YOURSELF &
YOUR NEIGHBORS IN PARKING-SPACE-POOR AREA A
WELL-DESERVED X-MAS PRESENT! CALL YOUR COUNCIL-
PERSONS OFFICE (SEE PG.2, GTE TEL.DIR.) & ASK
FOR YOUR SUPPORTING (ITEM 29) AT COUNCIL MEETING
ON TUES., DEC. 22, '87. ALL IT TAKES IS A TEL-
EPHONE CALL! CALL BETWEEN 8A.M. & 4P.M. 12/21
& 12/22.--THE CITY COUNCIL HAS ALREADY STOPPED
COLLECTION OF PARKING METER FEES & THUS, PARK-
ING TICKETS FOR OVERTIME PARKING IN ALL STREETS
& PUBLIC METERED PARKING LOTS IN BELMONT SHORE.)-
ANYWAY, IN ALL OVERCROWDED L.B. AREAS WITH MANY MORE RESIDENTS (& THEIR CARS)
AT HOME DUE TO THE HOLIDAYS, IT WILL BE IMPOSSIBLE FOR THE STREETSWEEPING
TRUCKS TO CLEAN THE GUTTERS.--LET'S TAKE A BROOM & CLEAN THE GUTTER AROUND OUR
OWN CAR(S) & GIVE THE METER-MAIDS & STREETSWEEPING TICKETERS A HOLIDAY VACA-
TION!--IF THIS MAKES SENSE, CALL YOUR COUNCILPERSON'S OFFICE; &,IF POSSIBLE,
EXPRESS YOURSELF IN PERSON ON TUES., 7P.M., CITY HALL, CEDAR/OCEAN.[FREE PARKING]
"GRASP", c/o DAN ROSENBERG, 1325 E. 7TH ST., #9, L.B.90815 (591-5793)

P.S. I AM NOT SANTA CLAUS! FLYERS, ETC. COST MONEY! PLEASE, HELP NOW!

#50 11-10-67

SAY NO TO UNFAIR PARKING TICKETS!

YOU AIN'T SEEN NOTHING YET—THE CITY IS BUY-
ING MORE LAND TO IMPOUND ANOTHER 2,000 CARS EACH
YEAR.—IF YOU ARE SICK & TIRED OF BEING VICTIM-
IZED BY YOUR OWN CITY, HELP ME & ALL OF US
IF YOU AGREE, CUT                NO        GET 1. WRITE OUT A CHECK TO THE CITY FOR THE FULL
ALONG DOTTED              MORE!      AMOUNT OF FINE; PLUS, A CHECK FOR $5.00 MADE OUT
LINES & TAPE THIS                   TO: DAN ROSENBERG; PLUS, ANY COMMENTS YOU WANT TO
CARD THIS                            REACH THE CITY COUNCIL. (INCLUDE YOUR NAME,ADDRESS
SEND ME COPIES OF YOUR UNFAIR TICKETS—PHONE & TELE. ) ——@ PHONE ME IF YOU WISH TO DISCUSS MY
OR SEND A SELF-ADDRESSED, STAMPED ENVELOPE TO THE   LEGAL PLANS. START AN IMMEDIATE SLOW DOWN ON ISSUING
ADDRESS BELOW.                                        UNFAIR PARKING TICKETS & THEN STOP THEM COMPLETELY

591-5793 Dan Rosenberg

3. IF YOU HAVE REASON TO DOUBT THE ACCURACY OF THE CITY'S STATEMENT: "DON'T
WORRY, NEW BUILDING STANDARDS PROVIDE ADEQUATE OFF-STREET PARKING,"PRINT &
SIGN YOUR NAME, ADDRESS, TELE. # ON THE BACK (ON ONE LINE) TO INDICATE THAT
YOU WANT THE COUNCIL TO PLACE AN EMERGENCY MORATORIUM ON APARTMENT BUILDING &
REDUCE THE NUMBER OF PERMITTED UNITS PER LOT TO TWO(R-2).

PLEASE, RETURN TO DAN ROSENBERG, 1325 E. 7th ST., LONG BCH., CA.90813 (591-5793)

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 1, 1988


John R. Calhoun
City Attorney
City of Long Beach
City Hall
333 West Ocean Boulevard
Long Beach, CA 90802

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-88-017

Dear Mr. Calhoun:

You have asked for advice concerning the activities of Dan
Rosenberg and the Grass Roots Action for Sane Planning
Organization (GRASP).

The Commission's Legal Division does provide written advice
to persons with questions about their duties under the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/ (Section 83114(b).) We do
not provide formal advice to third parties about another
person's duties unless the third party has been authorized to
request advice by the person whose duties are in question.
(Regulation 18329(b)(8)(B).) Therefore, we cannot provide
formal advice on the question you have raised. However, we can
provide general guidance to assist you in analyzing the
situation.

Elected officeholders, candidates for state and local
elected offices, and "committees" are subject to the Act's
campaign provisions. Section 82013 defines "committee" as:

[A]ny person or combination of persons who
directly or indirectly does any of the following:

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise
indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code
of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code
of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

John R. Calhoun
February 1, 1988
Page 2


     (a) Receives contributions totaling one thousand
dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar year;

     (b) Makes independent expenditures totaling one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar year;
or

     (c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand
dollars ($10,000) or more in a calendar year to or at
the behest of candidates or committees....

     A person (other than a candidate or officeholder) who
receives money for "political purposes" has received
"contributions," and must register and file disclosure reports
as a "recipient committee" under Section 82013(a).  Section
82015 and Regulation 18215 define "contribution."

     Regulation 18215 defines "a payment for political
purposes," in relevant part, as a payment:

          (1) For the purpose of influencing or attempting
to influence the action of the voters for or against
the nomination or election of a candidate or
candidates, or the qualification or passage of any
measure....

     To summarize, a person or group which is receiving money
must intend to spend the money for "political purposes" as
defined in the Act and Commission regulations in order to incur
any disclosure obligations under the Act's campaign disclosure
provisions.  "Political purposes" is defined to include efforts
to influence the action of voters for or against candidates or
ballot measures.

     If you have any questions, please give me a call at
(916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]

                                        Diane M. Griffiths
                                        General Counsel

Enclosed for your information and use are copies of three flyers Mr. Rosenberg recently distributed in the City of Long Beach which solicit contributions for what may be political purposes or activities regarding parking laws and regulations of the City of Long Beach.

In the past few weeks, Mr. Rosenberg has made several appearances before the City Council and has mentioned these flyers in his presentations.

Some of the obvious questions raised by this situation are as follows:

1. Is Mr. Rosenberg and/or the GRASP organization a committee under Section 82013 of the California Government Code?

2. Does money given pursuant to the solicitation contained in the flyers constitute a "contribution" under Government Code Section 82015, and does an expenditure of said funds constitute an "expenditure" under Section 82025 of the California Government Code?

3. What mechanism exists, if any, for determining when the $500 threshold set forth in Section 82013 has been reached or exceeded?

4. If any money received as a result of the subject solicitations by Rosenberg and/or GRASP constitutes a "contribution" under the Political Reform Act, does Section 84307 require that said funds be deposited in a separate account regardless whether the $500 threshold is reached?

Diane Griffith, General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
December 29, 1987
Page Two

     5.  Is there any applicable FPPC rule or regu-
lation requiring any reports or accounting for
committees that receive or spend less than $500 in
contributions?

     It is requested that you review this matter and
provide our office with your comments and advice concerning
the applicability of the Political Reform Act and/or any of
the Commission's rules and regulations. The above references
to statutory provisions are merely intended to point out cer-
tain apparent questions raised by this matter, but please do
not restrict your review or comments to the code sections
mentioned.

     If you have any questions or need additional
information or desire to discuss this matter, please contact
me at (213) 590-6319.  Thank you for your consideration and
assistance.


                                    [signature]
                                    JOHN R. CALHOUN
                                    CITY ATTORNEY

MAR 21 0 41 AM 88


904 Highway 96
Yreka, Calif. 96097
March 15, 1988


Fair Political Practices Commission
General Counsel Diane Griffiths
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, Calif. 95804

Dear Ms. Griffiths:

This morning I called your office and would like
confirmation regarding the following question:

In running my campaign is there anything in your
regulations that would find it improper to use the following
approach to campaigning: A Contest. For the first registered
voter in my district to send a card with the number of individual
campaign adds presented on the local radio station. First post
card drawn after election day submitted will receive a Savings
Bond (amount yet undetermined).

These adds will be promoting economic grow ideas held by
me the candidate, candidate qualifications, encouraging people
participation in government, ie voting.

It is my understanding I will have to check with other
agencies as this may come under several government jurisdictions
however, I would like your letter pertaining to your expertise.

Thank you very much.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Nancy E. Gregg,
Candidate, Supervisor District 4,
Siskiyou County

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 23, 1988


Nancy E. Gregg
904 Highway 96
Yreka, CA  96097

Re:  88-117

Dear Ms. Gregg:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform
Act was received on March 21, 1988 by the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact me directly at (916) 322-5662.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.  Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions,
or more information is needed, you should expect a response
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written
advice.  If more information is needed, the person assigned to
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to
advise you as to the information needed.  If your request is
for informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we
can.  (See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Sec. 18329).)

     You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [illegible]
                                        Jeanne Pritchard
                                        Chief
                                        Technical Assistance and Analysis
                                        Division

sections.question: Members of the Long Beach City Council have expressed concern regarding the applicability of the Political Reform Act of 1974 and regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission to Mr. Dan Rosenberg and/or the Grass Roots Action for Sane Planning (GRASP) organization which he purports to represent. The specific questions raised are: 1. Is Mr. Rosenberg and/or the GRASP organization a committee under Section 82013 of the California Government Code? 2. Does money given pursuant to the solicitation contained in the flyers constitute a 'contribution' under Government Code Section 82015; and does an expenditure of said funds constitute an 'expenditure' under Section 82025 of the California Government Code? 3. What rules exist, if any, for when the $500 threshold set in Section 2013 has been reached or exceeded? 5. Is there any applicable FPPC rule or regulation requiring any reports or accounting for committees that receive or spend less than $500 in contributions?
sections.conclusion: The Commission cannot provide formal advice on the questions raised because the City Attorney is a third party not authorized to request advice on another person's duties. However, general guidance is provided: Elected officeholders, candidates, and 'committees' are subject to the Act's campaign provisions. Section 82013 defines 'committee' as any person receiving contributions of $1,000+, making independent expenditures of $1,000+, or making contributions of $10,000+ in a calendar year. A person receiving money for 'political purposes' must register and file disclosure reports as a recipient committee. 'Political purposes' is defined to include efforts to influence voter action for or against candidates or ballot measures. Therefore, a person or group receiving money must intend to spend it for 'political purposes' as defined in the Act to incur disclosure obligations.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Dan Rosenberg and/or the GRASP organization soliciting contributions through flyers regarding parking laws constitute a 'committee' under the Political Reform Act requiring disclosure obligations, and what rules apply regarding the $500 threshold for committee registration and reporting.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC cannot provide formal advice to third parties without authorization from the person whose duties are in question, but provides general guidance that 'committees' are defined by contributions and expenditure thresholds of $1,000 or more, and that only money intended for 'political purposes' as defined by the Act triggers disclosure obligations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains incoming request letter dated December 29, 1987 and FPPC response letter dated February 1, 1988. Response indicates FPPC cannot give formal advice to third parties without authorization from the subject person (Rosenberg). Document includes three campaign flyers distributed by Rosenberg/GRASP regarding parking tickets. The $500 threshold mentioned in question 3 appears to be an error or reference to a different threshold, as the Act references $1,000 for committee definition.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Members of the Long Beach City Council have expressed concern regarding the applicability of the Political Reform Act of 1974 and regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission to Mr. Dan Rosenberg and/or the Grass Roots Action for Sane Planning (GRASP) organization which he purports to represent. The specific questions raised are: 1. Is Mr. Rosenberg and/or the GRASP organization a committee under Section 82013 of the California Government Code? 2. Does money given pursuant to the solicitation contained in the flyers constitute a 'contribution' under Government Code Section 82015; and does an expenditure of said funds constitute an 'expenditure' under Section 82025 of the California Government Code? 3. What rules exist, if any, for when the $500 threshold set in Section 2013 has been reached or exceeded? 5. Is there any applicable FPPC rule or regulation requiring any reports or accounting for committees that receive or spend less than $500 in contributions?

CONCLUSION: The Commission cannot provide formal advice on the questions raised because the City Attorney is a third party not authorized to request advice on another person's duties. However, general guidance is provided: Elected officeholders, candidates, and 'committees' are subject to the Act's campaign provisions. Section 82013 defines 'committee' as any person receiving contributions of $1,000+, making independent expenditures of $1,000+, or making contributions of $10,000+ in a calendar year. A person receiving money for 'political purposes' must register and file disclosure reports as a recipient committee. 'Political purposes' is defined to include efforts to influence voter action for or against candidates or ballot measures. Therefore, a person or group receiving money must intend to spend it for 'political purposes' as defined in the Act to incur disclosure obligations.

In the past few weeks, Mr. Rosenberg has made several appearances before the City Council and has mentioned these flyers in his presentations. Some of the obvious questions raised by this situation are as follows: 1. Is Mr. Rosenberg and/or the GRASP organization a committee under Section 82013 of the California Government Code? 2. Does money given pursuant to the solicitation contained in the flyers constitute a "contribution" under Government Code Section 82015; and does an expenditure of said funds constitute an "expenditure" under Section 82025 of the California Government Code? 3. What mechanism exists, if any, for determining when the $500 threshold set forth in Section 82013 has been reached or exceeded? 4. If any money received as a result of the subject solicitations by Rosenberg and/or GRASP constitutes a "contribution" under the Political
embedding.summary: The City of Long Beach requests FPPC advice regarding whether Dan Rosenberg and his GRASP organization are subject to campaign disclosure requirements under the Political Reform Act based on their fundraising activities for parking law advocacy. The FPPC declines to provide formal advice to the third-party city attorney but provides general guidance explaining the definition of 'committee' and 'political purposes' under the Act.
